flashcards.front: What are the three access specifiers in C++?
flashcards.back: Public, Private, and Protected. They control visibility of class members.

flashcards.front: Which members of a base class are accessible ONLY to the derived class?
flashcards.back: Protected members. They are not accessible to outside code but can be used by derived classes.
Public members of base class become public in derived class; protected members become protected; private members remain private (inaccessible directly).
All members of base class become private in derived class. Derived class cannot access them as protected.
Public and protected members of base class become protected in derived class; private members remain inaccessible.

flashcards.front: Can a derived class access private members of base class?
flashcards.back: No, private members cannot be accessed directly by derived class. Only through public/protected member functions of base class.

flashcards.front: What is the default access specifier for class inheritance in C++?
flashcards.back: Private inheritance is the default for classes. Public is the default for structs.

flashcards.front: How does protected access differ from private in terms of derived class access?
flashcards.back: Protected members are accessible to derived classes; private members are not accessible to derived classes.

flashcards.front: What happens to base class constructors in derived class?
flashcards.back: Derived class constructors must call a base class constructor. Base class constructors are not inherited but can be invoked.

flashcards.front: Can we change the access level of inherited members in derived class?
flashcards.back: Yes, using 'using' declaration. For example: using BaseClass::privateMember; to make it accessible in derived class.

flashcards.front: What is the order of constructor execution in inheritance?
flashcards.back: Base class constructor executes first, then derived class constructor. Destructor executes in reverse order.

flashcards.front: What is inheritance access control used for in OOP?
flashcards.back: It implements encapsulation by controlling what is exposed outside the class hierarchy. Helps in data hiding and information hiding.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between 'has-a' and 'is-a' relationship in inheritance?
flashcards.back: 'Is-a' uses public inheritance (Dog is a Animal). 'Has-a' uses composition (Car has a Engine).

flashcards.front: What is multiple inheritance in C++?
flashcards.back: A derived class inheriting from more than one base class. Requires careful access control for each base class.
When you want to hide the base class interface from external users but allow derived classes to access base functionality.
